text: The surgeon creates a single large flap across the surgical field, detaching the median umbilical ligaments in the process.
Two 12x16 pro group meshes are placed in the abdomen along with the necessary sutures. The surgeon begins the medial dissection, a technique typically used even for unilateral inguinal hernias.

The surgeon dissects down the midline, locating the pubis and Cooper's ligament. The dissection then shifts laterally, staying preperitoneal to preserve fat on the abdominal wall while searching for the cord structures.
The surgeon reduces a small direct hernia, avoiding dissection into the inguinal canal due to the absence of an indirect sac.
The surgeon observes the peritoneal flap without a hernia sac and proceeds with parietalization. A hole created during the procedure is visible but will be closed before the end of the surgery.

The surgeon carefully separates the bladder from the fat overlying the iliac vessels, maintaining a clear dissection plane. A brief search for a femoral hernia reveals mostly nodal tissue, prompting the surgeon to proceed with parietalizing the cord on the right side.

The surgeon identifies a sizable lipoma within the surgical field, which may account for the patient's symptoms. Careful manipulation is performed to reach the apex while ensuring the cord and testicle are not inadvertently displaced.

The surgeon continues to mobilize the lipoma, ensuring it is positioned far enough back to sit beneath the mesh without pulling up the inferior edge. The lipoma is fully mobilized without the need for amputation, allowing adequate space for mesh placement.

The surgeon moves to the left side, sweeping the cord structures down while observing the absence of an indirect sac. The dissection continues along the cord, revealing a small left-sided direct lipoma or direct hernia.

The surgeon identifies the plane between the bladder and the fat overlying the iliac vessels, prioritizing space for the inferior edge of the mesh. The space is developed to ensure the mesh fits snugly in the groove, hugging the wall along the iliac vessels while preserving the fat layer.

The surgeon searches for the left side of the lipoma, which is visibly present. The cord is carefully swept off the lipoma to avoid pulling the cord or testicle. A blood vessel emanating from within the canal is ligated to facilitate further reduction of the lipoma for mesh placement.
The surgeon fully reduces the lipoma, ensuring adequate space for mesh placement. The final view shows the surgical field prepared for the placement of a twelve by sixteen mesh on the right side.
The surgeon places the left-sided mesh over the previously mobilized area, ensuring it fits securely without tension on the surrounding structures.

The surgeon ensures the interior edges of the mesh oppose the sidewall to prevent tissue from sliding underneath. The flap is closed starting from the middle, then moving to the left and right. A small rent near the VAS joining the cord structures is repaired with a 3-0 Vicryl suture. The lipoma is placed back into the flap to sit on the inner surface of the mesh.
The surgeon places the right lipoma back into the flap and proceeds to close the surgical site, ensuring proper alignment for mesh placement.

The surgeon inserts an angiocath into the central flap and connects it to suction, using a grasper to compress the flap. This process removes gas and ensures the mesh remains flat without clamshelling or folding.
